coredata.dc:title: PSS functionalized and stabilized carbon nanodots for specific sensing of iron in biological medium
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Ghosh, Malabika
Dasgupta, Uddipan
Nayek, Sumanta
Saha, Abhijit
Bhattacharjee, Rama Ranjan
Chowdhury, Ankan Dutta
coredata.dc:description: Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) are already emerged as an excellent sensing element for its exceptional behavior in fluorescence, biocompatibility, and water dispersibility. However, its poor stability, selectivity and reproducibility in complex medium still be a big problem for its practical application. To overcome this, in the work, we have developed a new type of carbon quantum dot-PSS fluorescent nanocomposites which has been used for specific Fe3+ detection. The polystyrene sulfonate (PSS) polymer not only stabilize the QDs but also produces specific sites for Fe3+ to make a co-ordinate complex via Fe3+-SO3. The detection limit is calculated as low as 1 ppm which is adequate for measuring Fe3+ in blood or water samples. The mechanism of the quenching is very specific towards the Fe3+ ion due to the presence of PSS which makes the sensor selective among other metal ions and possible interferences. The rapid process of sensing, simple instrumentation, and excellent performances in presence of 1 % BSA and serum samples indicates the possible application for diagnostic usage in near future.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Biosensor
Stable quantum dots
Ferric ions detection
Optical sensor
Selective

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of Fe3+ using CQD-PSS nanocomposites
Synthesis and characterization of CQD-PSS nanocomposites:

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic presentation of CQD-PSS nanocomposites, (a) TEM image, (b) high magnified TEM image of CQD-PSS nanocomposites (inset) zoomed image of few CQDs, (c) excitation dependent fluorescence spectra of CQD-PSS nanocomposites, (d) images of CQD- PSS under day light and UV light of 360 nm, (e) images of bare CQD under day light and UV light of 360 nm, and (f) HRTEM image of bare CQD.
(a) RAMAN, (b) XRD, (c) FTIR and (d) DLS of CQD-PSS nanocomposites.

Fluorescence spectra of CQD-PSS at various concentrations of Fe3+ ranging from 0 to 100 ppm, at the excitation of 360 nm, (b) the change in fluorescence intensity as a function of Fe3+ concentration and (c) the images of change in fluorescence intensity of CQD-PSS solution in the presence of various concentrations of Fe3+ (5, 10, 20, 50 and 100 ppm) under the excitation of 360 nm UV light (extreme right cuvette contains bare CQD-PSS without Fe3+).

(a) Time dependent quenching of CQD-PSS nanocomposites over 1 h of incubation of 50 ppm Fe3+ ions, (b) response time calculation of the sensor, (c) selectivity of the CQD-PSS nanocomposites with various metal ions with 50 ppm concentration, (d) comparative bar diagram of Fe3+ detection with other interfering metal ions, and (e) effect of 1 % BSA on CQD-PSS and in presence of 100 ppm Fe3+ detection.
Effect of 1 % and 5 % serum on CQD-PSS and in presence of Fe3+ detection.
Preparation of Carbon Quantum Dots with Polystryrene sulphonate (CQD-PSS) nanocomposites and the proposed Mechanism for Fe3+ Detection.

head.ce:title: PSS functionalized and stabilized carbon nanodots for specific sensing of iron in biological medium

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Amity Institute of Nanotechnology, Amity University Kolkata, Major Arterial Road, AA II, Newtown, Kolkata, West Bengal 700135, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Amity Institute of Environmental Sciences, Amity University Kolkata, Major Arterial Road, AA II, Newtown, Kolkata, West Bengal 700135, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Amity Institute of Environmental Sciences, Amity University Kolkata, Major Arterial Road, AA II, Newtown, Kolkata, West Bengal 700135, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: UGC-DAE Consortium for Scientific Research, Kolkata Centre, Plot 8, Block LB, Sector III, Bidhannagar, Kolkata 700 106, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: UGC-DAE Consortium for Scientific Research, Kolkata Centre, Plot 8, Block LB, Sector III, Bidhannagar, Kolkata 700 106, India

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Current affiliation: Department of Chemistry, Sister Nivedita University, DG Block (Newtown), Action Area I, 1/2, Newtown, New Town, West Bengal 700156, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) are already emerged as an excellent sensing element for its exceptional behavior in fluorescence, biocompatibility, and water dispersibility. However, its poor stability, selectivity and reproducibility in complex medium still be a big problem for its practical application. To overcome this, in the work, we have developed a new type of carbon quantum dot-PSS fluorescent nanocomposites which has been used for specific Fe3+ detection. The polystyrene sulfonate (PSS) polymer not only stabilize the QDs but also produces specific sites for Fe3+ to make a co-ordinate complex via Fe3+-SO3. The detection limit is calculated as low as 1 ppm which is adequate for measuring Fe3+ in blood or water samples. The mechanism of the quenching is very specific towards the Fe3+ ion due to the presence of PSS which makes the sensor selective among other metal ions and possible interferences. The rapid process of sensing, simple instrumentation, and excellent performances in presence of 1 % BSA and serum samples indicates the possible application for diagnostic usage in near future.

ce:section.ce:para: Fe3+ is one of the transitional metal ions which is esteemed among the most essential metal ions in biological as well as water purification system [1–4]. Increasing the presence of Fe3+ ions in natural water bodies has become significant threat to human health as Fe3+ plays an essential role in physiological system involves several metabolic reactions, oxygen transportation, DNA synthesis [5,6]. So surplus or deficiency of iron in living body leads to different kind of disorders like anemia, arrythmia, liver and heart failure etc. [7,8]. The precise determination of low level of Fe3+ concentration becomes a very important diagnosis strategy to monitor biological pathways. Several conventional and advanced analytical techniques including spectrophotometry [9], atomic absorption spectrometry [10], inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry [11] and electrochemical [3,12] method are extremely good tools to determine the concentration of Fe3+. However, these methods require sophisticated instrumentations, costly complicated laboratory equipment and trained technicians for laborious sample preparation procedures. Though there are plenty research papers are already published on the metal ion sensor in last two decades, however, most of the sensors have a restricted performances in presence of complex medium, containing interferences. Fluorescence assays using standard fluorophores or quantum dots have become significant to achieve high sensitivity and rapid response to metal ions [13,14]. The simple instrumentation with high sensitivity makes the fluorescence method as a prime choice for metal ion sensing. Numerous fluorescent probes have been employed to date as notable examples of these usages, including metal nanoparticles [15,16], semiconductor quantum dots [17,18], and fluorescent organic dyes [19]. However, to avoid the toxic effect of organic dyes and other metal QDs, highly sensitive carbon quantum dots are emerging as the promising fluorophores for applications in the field of drug delivery, biosensing, bioimaging and metal ion sensing [20–22].

The carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a new emerging class of carbon nanomaterials which are zero dimensional, draw the attention of scientists due to its good fluorescence property, cheap precursors, easy synthetic route, minimal toxicity, and good photo-chemical stability. Depending on the synthesis procedures, the primary carbon atoms can exhibit different degrees of hybridizations producing crystalline and amorphous structures of the carbon quantum dots. Polymer passivated CQDs have some additional advantage like enhance fluorescence intensity, good stability as highly reactive functional groups are present beneath the polymer layer [23]. Easy dispersibility in water up to a large extent is one of the appealing properties of the carbon nano composites which exhibits good colloidal stability. The detection limits and the sensitivity of these sensors become impressive sometimes in standard buffer medium. But in presence of metal ions there is a high possibility of aggregation of particles. In addition, due to presence of plenty functional groups on the edge of the CQDs and the ring structures of the graphitic planes shows some general non-specific interaction with metal ions and other biomolecules, resulting poor selectivity for sensor application. Therefore, it will be a great outcome if the CQD can wrapped or protected with some polymer which can restrict the non-specific interaction from interferences, nullifying the interferences on graphitic electronic transition between their planes. Several efforts have been made on the functionalization of specific organic functional group at the edge of QDs to generate specificity. As example, dopamine moiety has been attached with graphene QDs for Fe3+ detection [24]. PEDOT/PSS nanocomposites has also been used with rGO’s graphitic planes for Fe3+ detection employing the coordination complex of Fe3+-PSS [25]. However, the flexible film forming ability and the protecting role of PSS has not received much attention. In this work, we have developed a new type of carbon quantum dot-PSS fluorescent nanocomposites which can be used for specific Fe3+ detection (Scheme 1 ). The carbon precursor of citric acid has been pyrolyzed with PSS polymer to make the successful polymer passivated CQDs which shows a good fluorescence property and quenching of fluorescence emission occurs in presence of Fe3+ metal ion.

Citric acid monohydrate (CA), AgNO3, CaCl2, Cr2(SO4)3, CuSO4. 5H2O, FeCl2, FeCl3, MnSO4, NiSO4, Pb(NO3)2, ZnSO4, Brovine Serum Albumin (BSA) were purchased from Shanghai Loba Chemie Pvt. ltd., Poly (Sodium 4-styrenesulphonate), Mw-10000000 (PSS) was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich, Fetal Bovine Serum was purchased from HiMedia and used as received without further purification. The double distilled water was used throughout for all experiments.

For the preparation of CQDs-PSS nanocomposite Citric acid and PSS were dissolved in water into two separate beakers in 5:1 (CA: PSS) weight ratio and stirred vigorously for 5–6 h. Both the solutions were mixed and kept on stirring for overnight. Then the solution was kept on water bath at 80 °C until a honey like substance was obtained. It was subsequently dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C and pyrolyzed at 200 °C for 4 h in muffle furnace. [26,27].
For control experiment the CQDs were prepared by using direct pyrolyzation of citric acid at 200 °C [24].

The high-resolution transmission electron microscopic (HRTEM) images were taken using a TECNAI G2 F20 microscope (FEI, America) at 200KV. The crystalline phase of samples was characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern recorded on Empyrean, Malvern Panalytical X-ray diffractometer with Cu-Kα radiation. The hydrodynamic diameter and zeta potential of the CQDs were measured by Dynamic light scattering (DLS) technique using Zetasizer Nano ZS, Malvern, UK instrument. Raman spectra were recorded using a Witec alpha 300 spectrometer. The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of CQDs-PSS were determined by using a Perkin Elmer Frontier FTIR. All fluorescence measurements were performed on a Hitachi F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer with excitation at 355 nm in quartz cell.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Fe3+ using CQD-PSS nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: For Fe3+ detection, 2 mL of diluted stock solution of CQDs were mixed with the aqueous Fe3+ solution to get the final concentration of 1 ppm-100 ppm. After 5 min, the change in fluorescence intensity was recorded under the excitation wavelength at 360 nm. In addition, the selectivity of sensing system was conducted by adding 7 different types of 100 ppm metal ion solutions into CQD solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of CQD-PSS nanocomposites:
ce:section.ce:para: Precursors of citric acid and PSS are mixed, and the quantum dots are produced due to the pyrolysis of citric acid in presence of PSS. At 200 °C, the self-conjugation of citric acid makes the graphitic carbon quantum dots, releasing the water molecules. As the PSS is quite stable at that temperature, it is anticipated that the CQD has incorporated on the matrix of PSS and formed the successful CQD-PSS nanocomposites, as depicted in the cartoon of Fig. 1 . The surface morphology of the as-prepared CQD-PSS was first characterized by TEM images. The TEM image of CQD-PSS shows the homogenous distribution (Fig. 1a) with a particle size of 10–25 nm (Fig. 1b). Few separated CQDs are clearly displayed in the inset of Fig. 1b which confirms the spherical shapes of QDs. Due to the low molecular weight, the existence of PSS layer is not visible in TEM images, however, the presence of lot of CQDs in a small area indicates the anchoring properties of a polymer in the nanocomposites. The fluorescence properties of CQD-PSS are also confirmed in Fig. 1c which shows the excitation dependent properties of fluorophores. The nanocomposite was excited from 280 nm to 420 nm of UV light where it shows the highest fluorescence intensity at the excitation of 360 nm which is the characteristic of ideal non-doped carbon quantum dots [24,28]. Fig. S1 shows the UV–vis absorption spectrum of CQD-PSS where the strong absorption peak is found at 320 nm, indicating the n–π* transition of CO [29]. The absorption bands at 220 and 232 nm are contributed from the π–π* transition [30]. Fig. 1d and 1e show the change in colour after visible and UV lights irradiations, respectively of the CQD-PSS nanocomposites and a bare CQDs, synthesized separately. The solution colour of bare CQDs is transparent under visible light irradiation, whereas the CQD-PSS nanocomposites shows light yellowish colour in visible light region. After irradiation of UV light at 360 nm, both emits in blue colour, confirming the successful synthesis of CQD-PSS nanocomposites. For comparison, the bare CQDs are also prepared by standard pyrolysis method from citric acid at 200 °C for 30 mins. The TEM image of bare GQDs is also showed in Fig. 1f.

In Fig. 2 a, the Raman spectra show graphitic nature of CQD-PSS nanocomposites. The nanocomposites display-two distinct D and G bands located at 1365 and 1605 cm−1 respectively, which are assigned to the characteristic peaks of graphitic planes [31]. The disordered (D) band at 1365 cm−1 is associated to the presence of sp3 defects, while the crystalline (G) band at 1605 cm−1 is attributed to the in-plane vibration of sp2 carbon. The ratio of intensities of these two bands (ID/IG) is found to be 0.68 for CQD-PSS nanocomposites. As the nanocomposites consists of a large number of PSS molecules with the RAMAN active CQD, the ratio is affected and down to 0.68. However, considering the small contribution of CQDs on the nanocomposites, it is good evidence of the existence of graphitic planes which is corroborated by the XRD result. In Fig. 2b, the broad peak at around 20.1° 2theta shown in the XRD pattern is the (002) plane of graphene, which is in good agreement with those results obtained from Raman spectra and HRTEM images [32]. Fig. 2c shows the FTIR spectra of CQD-PSS nanocomposites to understand the functional contribution of CQD. The broad peak at 3462 cm−1 is the stretching vibration of OH and NH bonds, which indicates the good hydrophilic nature of CQD-PSS nanocomposites [33]. The strong peak at 1683 cm−1 represents the CO stretching frequency in the carboxylic group of graphitic planes [34]. Formation of graphitic layer is confirmed by the strong peak of CH at 1365 cm−1 and the small hump of CC stretching at 2110 cm−1 [30].

DLS study of CQD-PSS (Fig. 2d) shows two different particle size distribution. The peak around 20 nm, supports the size of the nanocomposites, obtained from HRTEM data. However, a large distribution has also observed around 110 nm, as CQDs are prepared by high pressure thermal technique, few particles get aggregated due to interactions of the aromatic rings adsorbed on the surface of the CQD.

The surface and optical characterizations clearly show that the CQD-PSS nanocomposites have a superior and homogeneous size distribution pattern with small size and excellent stable fluorescence property which can be used for optical applications. Since the SO32- has a very strong affinity toward Fe3+, the CQD-PSS nanocomposites can be used for the iron detection purpose. A fluorescence measurement of CQD-PSS nanocomposites with Fe3+ was carried out in a PBS buffer solution at pH 7 and the change in fluorescence intensity was recorded after the incubation of 5 min. Fig. 3 a shows the change in fluorescence intensity of CQD-PSS nanocomposites solutions after addition of various concentrations of Fe3+ ranging from 1 ppm to 100 ppm. The quenching of fluorescence intensity of CQD-PSS nanocomposites increases with the increase in Fe3+ concentration. The sensing mechanism is mainly based on the specific interaction between Fe3+ and sulphite group on benzene ring of PSS which has been already reported in literatures [25,35,36]. There have been many reports on the quenching of fluorescence of CQDs in presence of Fe3+ [21,24,37,39]. It has been observed that in most literature reports the mechanism of quenching is not investigated in detailed or sometimes it is not supported with appropriate experimental data. In this work, the sensor has a selective binding towards Fe3+ compared to other cations which was already illustrated with experimental evidence. The PSS plays the crucial role for this sensing mechanism. Before addition of any metal ions, the high molecular weight polymer chains of PSS combine with multiple number of small dimensional CQDs. It creates a stable structure of CQD-PSS with very consistent fluorescence. In presence of Fe3+, few negatively charged sulphite groups make the co-ordination bonds with the small sized and high electric charged Fe3+ ions. Therefore, the electronic contribution of the benzene ring of PSS towards the graphitic planes of CQDs decreases, resulting neutralization of overall charge of CQD-PSS nanocomposites. DLS studies of the CQD-PSS, before and after Fe3+ addition, supports our hypothesis, presented in Fig. S2. Incorporation of Fe3+ within the colloidal bilayer of CQD-PSS that stabilizes the colloidal CQDs in aqueous medium. This is indicated by the fact at very low concentration of Fe3+ added (5 ppm), there is a vast increase in negative zeta potential (from –17 to –32 mV) of the colloidal suspension. Such increment in negative zeta value proves that the quenching in fluorescence is not related to the colloidal precipitation or destabilization of the colloidal CQDs. The phenomenon is also supported from the DLS size distribution before and after addition of Fe3+. As Fe3+ gets incorporated inside the fixed bilayer on the CQD surface, the Cl- counter ions get incorporated within the diffused layer surrounding the colloidal CQDs resulting in increased negative zeta potential. Hence the DLS size distribution of the CQD-PSS remain unchanged as shown in DLS and zeta experimental data.

Several studies have used the fluorescence property of graphene as the main detectable signal for detection of metal ions and organic molecules which are mainly focused on the adsorption of metal ions on the graphitic planes of CQDs. In those cases, the quenching of the fluorescence of bound CQDs is highly suffered from specificity due to the general adsorption of metal ions within the graphitic planes. In this work, use of the PSS polymer as the detecting molecules instead of direct graphitic planes makes the sensor more robust and specific towards target. To establish the role of PSS on the sensing mechanism, the detection of Fe3+ was also conducted with bare CQDs under identical conditions. As shown in Fig. S3, the original fluorescence intensity of bare CQD based probes are comparable with the CQD-PSS nanocomposites. However, after the addition of 50 ppm Fe3+ ions, the quenching of CQD-PSS nanocomposites is much prominent compared to the bare CQDs, which confirms the role of PSS on sensing mechanism. The sulphite group of PSS has the specific interaction with the Fe3+ ions [37,38]. The selectivity for bare CQDs system compromise largely due to the absence of any selective group in the nanocomposites. Several studies have indicated that the quenching of fluorescence intensity of as-prepared CQDs by Fe3+ is mainly due to the adsorption phenomenon on the surface. However, these result unspecific binding and poor selectivity. In this work, the result indicates that the quenching efficiency attributed to the adsorption of Fe3+ ions onto the surface of CQD-PSS which is specific for the anchoring group of PSS.

The calibration plot of absolute change in fluorescence intensity, % ΔF |ΔF| =(Fi-F0)/F0*100, where Fi = fluorescence intensity at ith time of measurement and F0 = initial fluorescence intensity of PSS-CQD) as a function of Fe3+ concentration is shown in Fig. 3b. The negative change (quenching) in fluorescence intensity increases rapidly with the increasing concentration of Fe3+. A good linear relationship between the % ΔF and Fe3+ concentration with a linear range between 1 ppm and 100 ppm is achieved and plotted in Fig. 3b. The linear regression equation of % ΔF = 5.76 + 0.75 × C 3+ with a correlation coefficient of 0.99 is also obtained. The limit of detection (LOD), determined by the 3δ/S (δ is the standard deviation of the lowest signal and S is the slope of linear calibration plot), is 8.1 ppm, which is below the range of blood and ground water Fe3+ concentration. The fluorescence images under the excitation of UV light at 360 nm of different Fe3+ concentration added CQD-PSS are shown in Fig. 3c. Photographs were taken just after the addition of various Fe3+ ions in the same solution of CQD-PSS and after 5 min when the quenching process was completely over.

The response time for the Fe3+ interaction with the CQD-PSS nanocomposites has been studied with various time interval (Fig. 4 a). As shown in Fig. 4b, the initial quenching effect was very fast whereas it almost saturated within 5 min of incubation. It confirms that the interaction between the Fe3+ ions with the CQD-PSS nanocomposites is very fast but quite stable over a long period of time (1 h). Therefore, all the sensing process, the incubation time was maintained for 5 mins.

Selectivity is one of the most important parameters for any biosensing application. The selectivity of CQD-PSS nanocomposites-based fluorescence sensor is presented in Fig. 4c. Like the detection of Fe3+, the quenching of fluorescence intensity (% ΔF) of CQD-PSS in the presence of 50 ppm metal ions, Fe2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Cr3+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Ag+ and Mn2+, was also analysed after incubation of 5 min. For easy understanding, the % ΔF for all metal ions are presented in bar diagram in Fig. 4d. Of all the ions tested, no one showed significant quenching effect on fluorescence intensity, due to lack of any selective group. In addition, due to the presence of PSS as the protective layer, the adsorption of metal ions on the graphitic surface becomes also negligible. It is noteworthy that sulphite group in PSS has high affinity toward small charged trivalent cations because of the high charge density. Due to its high selectivity, the efficiency of CQD-PSS was further examined in complex biological medium of 1 % BSA. It is good to observe that the interferences of large sized protein are almost negligible which is very rare for carbon based optical sensing. Moreover, the efficiency of the sensor is almost unaffected for Fe3+ detection in presence of BSA. After the examination in 1 % BSA, the proficiency of the CQD-PSS nanocomposites was further studied in different concentrations of Fe3+ spiked samples in 1 % and 5 % serum (Fig. 5 ). The effect of only serum on CQD-PSS is presented in Fig. S4, which shows a very nominal effect on sensor probes. In cases of iron-spiked serum samples, the CQD-PSS nanocomposites shows increasing quenching effect with increasing concentration of Fe3 + similar like water spiked samples. The results clearly confirm the protecting nature of PSS on the fluorophore of CQD from the interferences of serum. It is clear from the above selectivity experiments that the CQD-PSS based sensing probes possess high selectivity which can effectively detect Fe3+ specifically from the mixture that contains all possible competitive metal ions and interferences.

In this study, we have successfully synthesized a new type of PSS combined CQDs by in- situ synthesis of carbon dots. The successfully synthesized nanocomposite between CQD and the polymeric structure of PSS provides a robust platform of non-interactive composites with any adsorbents having stable fluorescence. Using the specific but strong complex interaction between sulphite group of PSS and Fe3+, the CQD-PSS nanocomposites has been used for the detection of Fe3+ in the range of 1 to 100 ppm with the detection limit of 8.1 ppm. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first time that CQDs are synthesized with PSS for the detection of Fe3+, employing the protective role and the specific interaction of PSS for optical sensing. The excellent linearity of calibration curve and the selectivity of Fe3+ among the other competitive metal ions and most importantly in BSA and serum medium, make CQD-PSS nanocomposites an excellent sensing probe for on-time iron detection.

Malabika Ghosh: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation. Uddipan Dasgupta: Methodology, Investigation. Sumanta Nayek: Resources. Abhijit Saha: Resources. Rama Ranjan Bhattacharjee: Supervision. Ankan Dutta Chowdhury: Conceptualization, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: R.R.B thanks to UGC-DAE-CSR Kolkata center for providing facilities including DLS under the project UGC- DAE-CSR-KC/CRS/RC09/0994/1029. S.N and R.R.B are thankful to DST, Government of India, (Project No. DST/TMD/EWO/WTI/2K19/EWFH/2019/217) for providing research facilities and financial support. We sincerely thanks to Dr. Goutam Pramanik of IUC Kolkata for light scattering experiments (DLS). A.D.C sincerely thanks to Department of Biotechnology for providing Ramalingaswami fellowship (Grant No. BT/RLF/Re-entry/67/2020).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2023.122445.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

Biol. Trace Elem. Res.

Sci. Rep.
